title: JSX Unveiled - HTML in JavaScript
description: Master JSX syntax and learn how to write markup inside JavaScript.
longDescription: JSX is the special syntax that makes React components look like HTML. In this lesson, you'll discover what JSX really is, learn its rules and quirks, and practice converting between HTML and JSX. By the end, writing JSX will feel natural!

learningObjectives.statement: Explain what JSX is and why it exists
Write valid JSX with correct syntax
Convert HTML to JSX and vice versa

missionNarrative.title: The JSX Transformation
missionNarrative.story: Deep in Component Cove lies the ancient JSX Scroll - a magical artifact that transforms JavaScript into beautiful UI. Legend says only those who master its syntax can unlock React's full power. Time to become a JSX wizard!

sections.title: What is JSX?
sections.content:
**JSX** (JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for JavaScript that lets you write HTML-like markup directly in your JavaScript code.

**Why JSX?**
- Makes components **readable** - structure is visual
- Keeps logic and markup **together** - related code stays close
- Gets **compiled** to regular JavaScript by tools like Babel
- Feels like HTML but has **superpowers**

JSX isn't required for React, but it's the standard way everyone writes React!

sections.title: JSX vs JavaScript
JSX is an Expression
JSX Rules & Differences from HTML
sections.content:
## Key JSX Rules ### 1. `className` instead of `class` Since `class` is a reserved word in JavaScript: ```javascript // ❌ Wrong - class is reserved
Content
// ✅ Correct
Content
``` ### 2. All Tags Must Close Unlike HTML, every tag needs closing: ```javascript // ❌ HTML style
// ✅ JSX requires self-closing
``` ### 3. CamelCase Attributes HTML attributes become camelCase: ```javascript // HTML → JSX tabindex → tabIndex onclick → onClick onchange → onChange for → htmlFor maxlength → maxLength ``` ### 4. Wrap in Curly Braces for JavaScript Use `{}` to embed JavaScript expressions: ```javascript const name = "Alice"; const age = 25;

Hello, {name}!

You are {age} years old

Next year: {age + 1}

Adult: {age >= 18 ? 'Yes' : 'No'}

```

sections.title: JavaScript Expressions in JSX
What You CAN'T Do in JSX
Text Content in JSX
sections.content:
## Handling Text and Whitespace ### Escaping Curly Braces To display literal `{` or `}`: ```javascript // Show {name} as text

{{ '{{name}}' }}

{/* or use HTML entities */}

{name}

``` ### Whitespace Handling JSX condenses whitespace: ```javascript // These are equivalent:
Hello World
Hello World
Hello{' '}World
{/* Add space explicitly */} ``` ### Comments in JSX Use `{/* comment */}` syntax: ```javascript
{/* This is a JSX comment */}

Title

{/* Multi-line comment */}
```

challenges.title: HTML to JSX Conversion
challenges.description: Convert HTML attributes to JSX syntax
challenges.question: Which is the correct JSX conversion?
challenges.instructions: Select the correct JSX version for each HTML snippet.

options.explanation: Wrong! 'class' is a reserved word in JavaScript.
Nope! Self-closing tags use />, not full closing tags.
Correct! Self-closing tags need the slash.
Correct! Events are camelCase and use curly braces!

challenges.successFeedback: Great job! You're mastering JSX syntax!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: className, self-closing tags, and camelCase events!

hints.text: Self-closing tags need a forward slash before the closing bracket.
Event handlers are camelCase and wrapped in curly braces.

hints.finalExplanation: JSX uses className instead of class, requires self-closing tags with />, and uses camelCase for events like onClick.

challenges.title: Expression or Statement?
challenges.description: Identify what can be used inside JSX curly braces
challenges.question: Which of these can be used inside JSX { }?
challenges.instructions: Select ALL valid expressions that work in JSX.

options.explanation: Correct! Property access is an expression.
Wrong! if statements can't go directly in JSX.
Function declarations are statements, not expressions.
Correct! Array methods return values, making them expressions!

challenges.successFeedback: Perfect! You know the difference between expressions and statements!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: expressions return values, statements don't.

hints.text: Expressions produce a value. Statements perform an action.
Ternary operators (?:) are expressions. if/else are statements.

challenges.title: Fix the JSX Errors
challenges.description: Debug and fix invalid JSX code
challenges.question: Fix all JSX errors in this component
challenges.instructions: Fill in the blanks to correct the JSX syntax errors.
challenges.failureFeedback: Check for: className, proper variable embedding, self-closing tags, and comment syntax.

hints.text: Self-closing tags like img need a / before the closing >.

challenges.title: Build a JSX Component
challenges.description: Create a component using JSX with embedded expressions
challenges.question: Create a ProductCard component
challenges.instructions: Complete the ProductCard component that displays product name, price, and whether it's in stock using JSX expressions.
challenges.successFeedback: Awesome! Your JSX component looks great!
challenges.failureFeedback: Make sure to use className, embed variables with {}, and use a ternary for conditional display.

hints.text: Wrap your content in a div with className='product-card'.
Use curly braces to embed variables: {productName}
Use a ternary operator for the inStock status: {inStock ? 'In Stock' : 'Out of Stock'}
Don't forget to close all your tags properly!

reflectionQuestion.question: What do you think is the biggest advantage of JSX over writing plain HTML and JavaScript separately? Describe a scenario where JSX makes your code cleaner.
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: JSX keeps related code together. For example, when building a user profile card, I can keep the user data logic right next to the markup that displays it. Without JSX, I'd have to jump between my JS file and HTML file, or use verbose createElement calls.

resources.title: React Docs - Writing Markup with JSX
